title: Verbs — Present Tense
description: Verbs are the engine of every sentence. German verbs change their ending to match who is doing the action. Learn one pattern of endings and you can conjugate hundreds of verbs.

lessons.title: Regular Verbs — One Set of Endings
lessons.subtitle: machen, spielen, lernen — chop off -en, add the ending for the person.
lessons.hook: English is lazy with verbs: I play, you play, we play — only 'he plays' adds anything. German is more organized: every person gets its own ending. That sounds like more work, but it's actually a gift — the ending tells you WHO is acting, even in a fast or scrambled sentence.
lessons.explain: A German verb has two parts: a stem and an ending. Take the infinitive (spielen = to play), remove -en to get the stem (spiel-), then add the ending for the subject: ich spiele, du spielst, er/sie/es spielt, wir spielen, ihr spielt, sie/Sie spielen. That's the full set: -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en. This exact pattern works for the huge majority of German verbs — machen, lernen, wohnen, kaufen, and thousands more. Memorize the six endings once; reuse them forever.

vocabulary.memoryHook: ich mache, du machst, er macht. The most useful verb for everyday questions.
spiel- + endings. Sounds like 'spiel' in English board-game culture.
lern- + endings. You're doing it right now: du lernst Deutsch.
For where you LIVE (reside). ich wohne, du wohnst, er wohnt.
komm- + endings. 'Woher kommst du?' is a first-conversation must.

exercises.explanation: For 'du', the ending is -st: du lernst. Stem (lern-) + -st. This -st ending only ever belongs to 'du'.

exercises.prompt: Which ending belongs to 'er / sie / es'?
exercises.explanation: er/sie/es takes -t: er macht, sie spielt, es kommt. Note 'ihr' (you all) also uses -t, but that comes with the plural set. The singular he/she/it always ends in -t.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Wir ___ im Park.' (We play in the park.) — verb: spielen
exercises.hint: The 'wir' ending is -en (same as the infinitive).
exercises.explanation: For 'wir', the ending is -en: wir spielen. Handily, wir and sie/Sie both use the full -en form, identical to the infinitive.

lessons.title: Sein and Haben — The Two You Can't Live Without
lessons.subtitle: 'to be' and 'to have' are irregular — and everywhere. Learn them cold.
lessons.hook: If you learn only two verbs today, make them sein (to be) and haben (to have). 'I am tired', 'I have a question', 'she is nice', 'we have time' — you can't survive a single conversation without them. They're irregular, so they don't follow the tidy pattern from Lesson 1. That's exactly why they're worth memorizing outright.
lessons.explain: sein (to be) is the most irregular verb in German: ich bin, du bist, er/sie/es ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie/Sie sind. haben (to have) is mostly regular but drops the 'b' in du and er forms: ich habe, du hast, er/sie/es hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie/Sie haben. Don't try to derive these — just drill them until they're automatic. They also power future grammar (perfect tense uses haben and sein), so this investment pays off for months.

vocabulary.memoryHook: bin, bist, ist, sind, seid, sind. Fully irregular — memorize as a chant.
habe, hast, hat, haben, habt, haben. The 'b' vanishes in du hast / er hat.

vocabulary.exampleTranslation: In the evening I am tired.
vocabulary.memoryHook: Adjective. Pairs constantly with sein: ich bin müde.
die Zeit. 'Hast du Zeit?' — the ultimate friendly opener.

exercises.explanation: sein → ich bin. This form only ever goes with 'ich'. 'bist' is du, 'ist' is er/sie/es.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Er ___ einen Hund.' (He has a dog.)
exercises.explanation: haben → er hat. In the du and er/sie/es forms, haben loses its 'b': du hast, er hat. Everywhere else the 'b' stays (wir haben, ihr habt).

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct 'wir' form of sein?
exercises.explanation: sein → wir sind. Careful: 'ihr seid' (you all) is spelled differently and is a classic trap. wir/sie/Sie = sind; ihr = seid.

lessons.title: Stem-Changing Verbs — When the Vowel Shifts
lessons.subtitle: fahren → er fährt, lesen → er liest. A small vowel change in du and er.
lessons.hook: Some very common verbs pull a small trick: their stem vowel changes — but ONLY in the du and er/sie/es forms. 'fahren' (to drive) becomes 'du fährst, er fährt'. It looks scary, but the pattern is tight and predictable: a→ä, e→ie, e→i. Once you spot it, you'll recognize it everywhere.
lessons.explain: Stem-changing (or 'strong') verbs keep the normal endings from Lesson 1 — but their stem vowel shifts in the du and er/sie/es forms only. The three common shifts are: a → ä (fahren: du fährst, er fährt), e → ie (lesen: du liest, er liest), and e → i (sprechen: du sprichst, er spricht). All other forms (ich, wir, ihr, sie) keep the original vowel. So it's not chaos — it's two forms per verb that differ, and always in the same predictable direction.

vocabulary.memoryHook: a → ä: ich fahre, du fährst, er fährt. ich and wir keep the plain 'a'.
e → ie: ich lese, du liest, er liest. Note du and er look the same (liest).
e → i: ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht. Essential for any conversation.
e → i: ich esse, du isst, er isst. (du isst and er isst look identical.)
der Zug — you'll need it in Chapter 6 at the train station.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Er ___ nach München.' (He drives to Munich.) — verb: fahren
exercises.hint: a → ä in the er form.
exercises.explanation: fahren is a stem-changer: a → ä in du and er/sie/es. So er fährt (with ä). 'fahrt' without the umlaut would be wrong for 'er'.

exercises.prompt: In stem-changing verbs, which forms change the vowel?
exercises.explanation: Only the du and er/sie/es forms change the stem vowel. ich, wir, ihr, and sie/Sie keep the original vowel. That's why it's just two forms to watch per verb.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Du ___ Deutsch.' (You speak German.) — verb: sprechen (e → i)
exercises.hint: e → i, then add the du ending -st.
exercises.explanation: sprechen changes e → i in du/er, then takes the normal -st for du: du sprichst. Stem-change first, then the regular ending.
